paper_title: The Hamilton-Jacobi Theory of Deep Learning

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: In this paper, training a neural network is identified, exactly, as a search through Hamilton--Jacobi initial-value problems: each gradient step selects the initial data of a viscous Hamilton--Jacobi equation whose Hopf--Cole propagator best fits the observations; at inference, the input is the spatial point at which that solution is evaluated and the initial condition is already encoded in the weights. The correspondence is exact for log-sum-exp layers and structural for broader architectures: residual networks, transformers, and recurrent architectures (RNNs, LSTMs, SSMs) each discretize the same class of Hamilton--Jacobi equations, with architecture-dependent Hamiltonian and viscosity. A single deformation parameter $\varepsilon$ unifies all four perspectives (network, tropical algebra, viscous PDE, convex optimization) in a commutative diagram closed under Lipschitz conditions. Quantitative consequences include: the minimax optimal generalization rate $O(n^{-1/(d+2)})$ for fixed $t$; adversarial robustness controlled by $\varepsilon$; backpropagation as the co-state equation of the Hamiltonian system for residual networks (Pontryagin Maximum Principle); scaling exponents consistent with data intrinsic dimension via PDE quadrature; and a closed-form $O(N)$ influence function (softmax attribution weights $π_j$) whose entropy landscape undergoes fold bifurcations as $\varepsilon$ increases, each merging attribution basins.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: What equation does neural network solve? Conventionally the question runs the other direction: given partial differential equation, design network to approximate its solution [Han et al., 2018]. Here trained network already is Hamilton Jacobi equation, and the question is which one. The key is single deformation parameter ε and ultradiscretization [Tokihiro et al., 1996]: the exact passage ε 0 between two algebraic worlds. At ε = 0, addition is max and multiplication is + (the tropical semiring); at ε > 0, ordinary arithmetic is restored and the same objects reappear as the solution operator of viscous PDE. The passage is not an approximation; it is an exact semiring homomorphism, the Maslov dequantization [Litvinov, 2007]. The mathematical object realizing this is layer with log-sum-exp activation: fε(x) = ε log (cid:88) j= (cid:16)(cid:0)Wj + bj exp (cid:1)/ε (cid:17) . (1) The algebraic structure of (1) is fixed by its tropical limit. At ε = 0, (1) collapses to maxj(Wj x+bj), the HopfLax formula and convex optimization problem. The passage ε 0 is the Maslov dequantization [Litvinov, 2007]: an exact semiring homomorphism from (R, +, ) to (R, max, +), not numerical approximation. Lifting ε above zero reverses this limit, converting the tropical machine into the smooth layer (1); the HopfCole linearization [Hopf, 1950] then identifies (1) exactly as the heat-equation propagator of viscous HamiltonJacobi equation (Theorem 4.1). The Preprint. weights encode the initial data, the architecture encodes the Hamiltonian, and forward pass evaluates that PDE solution at the query point x. The parameter ε appears simultaneously as the softmax temperature, the PDE viscosity, and the convex regularization strength; Theorem 7.1 shows these roles are identified, not coincidental. Contributions. The contribution is the unification: individual ingredients (Maslov dequantization [Litvinov, 2007], HopfCole linearization [Hopf, 1950], ResNet-as-ODE [E, 2017, Chen et al., 2018], adjoint method [Chen et al., 2018], scaling laws [Kaplan et al., 2020]) are known; connecting them under single ε yields commutative diagram and quantitative consequences that follow from none alone. Concretely, the paper establishes: (i) The LSE activation is smooth deformation of the tropical max operation; the limit ε 0 is an exact semiring homomorphism from (R, +, ) to (R, max, +) (Theorem 3.1). (ii) Every LSE-activated feedforward layer encodes the exact HopfCole solution of viscous HamiltonJacobi PDE under discrete measure (Theorem 4.1); transformer attention is the expected value vector under the Gibbs measure (Proposition H.9); deep networks correspond structurally to the PDE semigroup under point-evaluation composition. The tropical limit ε 0 recovers the HopfLax inf-convolution (Theorem 5.1), simultaneously linear program and MASO; ResNets and recurrent architectures (RNNs, LSTMs, SSMs) discretize the ODE characteristics with architecture-dependent viscosity (Propositions 5.2, 5.4). (iii) single parameter ε indexes four perspectives on the same object (neural network, tropical algebra, PDE, convex optimization) and the resulting commutative diagram (Theorem 7.1) closes under Lipschitz and convex H. (iv) The framework yields actionable design principles: optimal temperature ε 1/d prescribed from width and data dimension achieves minimax rate O(n1/(d+2)) (Theorem 8.1); adversarial robustness is certifiably controlled by ε (Corollary 8.2); backpropagation is the co-state equation of the Hamiltonian system for residual networks (Theorem 8.4); and the framework extends to learnable quadratic Hθ(p) = pAθp (Theorem 4.4). (v) The softmax weights πj are closed-form O(N ) influence function with exact label sensitivity ˆf /gj = πj(1+( ˆf gj)/ε) requiring no Hessian inversion; the attribution-entropy landscape H(π) undergoes fold bifurcations as ε increases, each annihilating an attribution basin, with saddle points marking attribution transitions (Appendix F). Together, the results constitute unifying mathematical theory of deep learning: Maslovs dequantization the same principle that connects classical to quantum mechanics here connects tropical to smooth neural computation, and activation type, architectural class, generalization, robustness, training dynamics, and scaling laws ordinarily studied in isolation emerge as facets of one commutative diagram, closed under Lipschitz and convex H. The exact HopfCole correspondence is complete for the quadratic Hamiltonian class (Theorems 4.1, 4.3, 4.4); structural correspondences extend to broader architectures. Notation. For ε > 0 and RN , define LSEε(z) = ε log (cid:80) exp(zi/ε). Write tr for tropical matrix multiplication: (A tr x)i = maxj(Aij + xj). For convex Hamiltonian : Rd R, let L(v) = supp(p H(p)) denote its Legendre transform. Write for inf-convolution: (f g)(x) = inf y{f (y) + g(x y)}.

sections.title: 2 Background
sections.content: Log-Sum-Exp and Convex Duality. The function LSEε : Rm is convex, smooth for ε > 0, and satisfies LSEε(x) = softmax(x/ε). Its LegendreFenchel conjugate is the negative entropy: LSE pi log pi on the simplex. It is simultaneously the log-partition function of the Gibbs distribution at temperature ε, the smooth convex relaxation of max, and, via the HopfCole substitution, the solution operator of the heat equation. ε(p) = ε (cid:80) The Tropical Semiring. The tropical semiring (R {}, , ) has = max(a, b) and = + b, with identities and 0 respectively. Tropical matrix multiplication acts as (A tr x)i = maxj(Aij + xj). Max-plus algebra and the tropical semiring are two names for the same structure [Litvinov, 2007]. 2 HamiltonJacobi PDEs. The viscous HamiltonJacobi (HJ) equation is tu + H(xu) = εxu, u(x, 0) = g(x), (2) with Hamiltonian : Rd and viscosity ε > 0. The inviscid equation has ε = 0. Under the HopfCole substitution = ε log v, equation (2) linearizes to the heat equation tv = εv for the quadratic Hamiltonian H(p) = p2 [Hopf, 1950, Evans, 2010]; for general convex the substitution yields non-linear equation for v. Viscosity solutions of the inviscid equation are characterized by CrandallLions theory [Crandall and Lions, 1983]; for convex the unique viscosity solution is given by the HopfLax formula [Lax, 1957, Evans, 2010]. Ultradiscretization. Ultradiscretization [Tokihiro et al., 1996] passes smooth system to maxplus system via ε log(eA/ε + eB/ε) max(A, B) as ε 0, and the reverse lift re-quantizes tropical system by raising ε above zero. Originally developed for soliton PDEs [Tokihiro et al., 1996], the same principle applies here to neural networks.

sections.title: 3 From Neural Networks to Max-Plus Algebra
sections.content: LSE as Deformation. For all Rm: maxi xi LSEε(x) maxi xi + ε log m. As ε 0 this sandwiches to max. The Maslov dequantization makes this more precise. Theorem 3.1 (Maslov dequantization [Litvinov, 2007]). For all Rm, lim xi. ε0 For each ε > 0, (R, LSEε, +) = (R0, +, ); the limit ε 0 is the semiring homomorphism onto the tropical semiring (R, max, +), under which addition becomes max and multiplication becomes +. LSEε(x) = max The passage ε 0 is formally analogous to the ℏ 0 limit in quantum mechanics: the same deformation of real arithmetic semiring into tropical one [Litvinov, 2007]. Two Regimes of Network Layer. At ε > 0: the affine-plus-LSE layer fε(x) = LSEε(W + b) is smooth; all neurons contribute with softmax weights; the output is the solution of an entropyregularized optimization. At ε = 0: the layer becomes f0(x) = maxj(Wj + bj), tropical linear map in which single neuron dominates. This is max-affine spline operator (MASO) [Balestriero and Baraniuk, 2018, 2021] that partitions the input into polyhedral regions, exactly as decision tree [Aytekin, 2022]. Softmax weights at ε > 0 are the continuous relaxation of the one-hot region indicator. Remark 3.2. ReLU networks arise at ε = 0 via the two-neuron comparison max(Wj x, 0) [Plate et al., 2026]. This is the tropical semiring operation: at ε = 0, addition becomes max and the log-sum-exp collapses to pairwise comparison. Softplus at ε > 0 is the same operation at nonzero point of the one-parameter family; the deformation ε interpolates continuously between the two. The passage ε 0 therefore amounts to taking the tropical limit of the smooth activation.

This object is, viz., simultaneously: (i) tropical inner product in (R, min, +); (ii) MASO [Balestriero and Baraniuk, 2018]; (iii) linear program in [Balestriero and Baraniuk, 2021]; and (iv) piecewise-affine function with breakpoints at the support points {yj}. ResNets and ODE Characteristics. ResNet layer xl+1 = xl + (xl, Wl) is the Euler discretization of x(t) = (x(t), θ(t)) [He et al., 2016, Lu et al., 2018, Chen et al., 2018, E, 2017]. Proposition 5.2 (ResNet as HJ characteristics). The ResNet recurrence with step size converges, as 0 and with Lh = fixed, to the solution of = (x, θ(t)). This ODE is the characteristic equation of the HJ PDE with Hamiltonian H(x, p) = (x, θ): characteristics satisfy = pH = (x, θ) and = xH [Chen et al., 2018, Tong et al., 2025]. The construction applies to any smooth recurrence of the form xℓ+1 = xℓ + hF (xℓ, Wℓ), with the characteristic equations determined by and θ. In the tropical limit, the residual layer becomes xl+1 = max(xl, Wl tr xl), tropical residual whose continuous limit is the inviscid characteristic equation. 5 Remark 5.3 (Intrinsic vs. extrinsic Hamiltonians). For LSE layers H(p) = p2 arises intrinsically (completing the square forces the Gaussian heat-kernel structure); for ResNets H(x, p) = (x, θ) is extrinsic any smooth ODE is HJ characteristic making the framework foundational rather than LSE-specific. Theorem 4.1 (exact) and Proposition 5.2 (asymptotic, 0) are complementary; Theorem 8.4 holds exactly at finite as discrete chain-rule identity (see Appendix H). Flow matching [Lipman et al., 2023, Liu et al., 2023] and score-based diffusion [Song et al., 2021] fit the same structure (Appendix B). Recurrent Architectures: RNNs, LSTMs, and SSMs. Recurrent and state-space architectures fit the same framework through the process-discretization route of Proposition 5.2. Proposition 5.4 (Recurrent architectures as HJ characteristics). The following structural correspondences hold: (a) RNNs. The recurrence ht = σ(Whht1 + Wxxt) is the unit-step Euler discretization of = + σ(Whh + Wxx(t)), the characteristic ODE of the HJ PDE with Hamiltonian H(h, p) = (h + σ(Whh + Wxx)). (b) State Space Models (SSMs). The linear SSM h(t) = A(t)h(t) + B(t)x(t), y(t) = C(t)h(t) is the characteristic equation of linear HJ PDE with quadratic Hamiltonian H(h, p) = pA(t)h + pB(t)x. Time-dependent matrices A(t), B(t), C(t) (as in Mambas selective scan) make the characteristics non-autonomous, the same structure as Tong et al. [2025]. The tropical limit of the SSM is max-plus linear system ht+1 = tr ht tr xt, the algebra used in scheduling and discrete-event systems. Remark 5.5 (LSTMs: gated, time-dependent characteristics). Since tanh(x/ε) = xLSEε(x, x)  (Table 6)  , LSTM gates are gradients of two-neuron LSE layers; the continuous-time cell equation is the HJ characteristic ODE with gated, time-dependent Hamiltonian (full expression in Appendix E). In the tropical limit, sigmoid Heaviside and tanh sign, so gates become {0, 1}d selectors and the cell update becomes max-plus switching automaton. The key distinction across architectures is how viscosity enters: in feedforward networks ε is global (uniform softmax temperature); in SSMs it is input-dependent (A(t) controlled by x(t)); in LSTMs it is gated per cell (Remark 5.5). The LSE class is further closed under composition, affine input transformations, and residual connections (Appendix H).

sections.title: 6 The ε Parameter: A Unifying Deformation
sections.content: The parameter ε simultaneously indexes four perspectives on the same object: neural network (ε > 0: LSE/softmax; ε = 0: max/ReLU), tropical algebra ((R, +, ) vs. (R, max, +)), PDE (viscous HJ vs. inviscid HJ), and convex optimization (entropic regularization vs. LP vertex). The full dictionary is in Table 5 (Appendix H). Infinite-width and overparameterization consequences (Gaussian process connection, double-descent as near-shock formation) are developed in Appendix E.

The complementary regime (x)/ε log corresponds to stochastic parrot [Bender et al., 2021] behavior. Assumption 8.7 (Manifold hypothesis). The data-generating measure µ on Rd is supported on (or concentrated near) Riemannian submanifold of intrinsic dimension deff d. The effective dimension deff equals when µ is non-degenerate in all directions, and equals the submanifold dimension when µ concentrates on lower-dimensional structure. 8 Proposition 8.8 (Scaling laws from intrinsic dimension). Under the approximation bound of Theorem 8.1, the training loss satisfies L(N ) 1/deff , where deff is the intrinsic dimension of the support of the data-generating measure µ. Equivalently, the empirical scaling law exponent α in L(N ) α satisfies α = 1 deff , deff = 1 α . (19) Proof sketch. See Appendix E. Table 7 (Appendix I) estimates deff = 1/α from published scaling exponents [Kaplan et al., 2020, Henighan et al., 2020, Hoffmann et al., 2022]; values range from deff 2.6 (math) to 13 (GPT-scale language). Attribution Landscape and Bifurcation. Theorem 8.9 (Fold bifurcations of the attribution-entropy landscape). Let H(x; ε) = (cid:80) πj log πj be the attribution entropy. Assume {yj} are distinct and each yk is the strict HopfLax minimizer at itself: g(yk) < g(yj) + yk yj2/(4t) for all = (generic). As ε 0, develops local minima at {yj}; as ε increases, each decrease in critical-point count is fold bifurcation where saddle annihilates an adjacent minimum at εbif (j, k), merging the attribution basins of and (generic: simple zero eigenvalue of 2 xH with nonzero crossing derivative). sections.title: 9 Numerical Experiments
sections.content: The core identity (Theorem 4.1) and two quantitative consequences (Theorem 8.1, Proposition 8.8) are verified numerically; full details and code are in Appendix G. Identity verification. The identity LSEε(W + b) = x2/(4t) uε(x, t) holds to machine precision ( 1016) for five values of ε across 500 evaluation points (Table 2, Appendix G). The transformer attention identity (7) holds to exactly 0 error across 500 random trials for {4, 8, 16, 32, 64} (Table 3, Appendix G). Quadrature convergence. Figure 5 (Appendix G) shows ℓ error for g(y) = (Lipschitz): all curves decay as O(N 1/d), confirming Theorem 8.1 across {1, 2, 4}. Scaling law. Figure 1 verifies Proposition 8.8: at optimal ε = 1/deff the RMSE scales as α with α = 1/deff ; fitted ˆα > 1/deff confirms the smooth-g rate exceeds the minimax Lipschitz bound. Figure 6 (Appendix G) replicates these exponents on Adamtrained networks. 1: Scaling Figure law L(N ) α at optimal ε; g(y) = 1 2 y2. Fitted ˆα > 1/deff confirms smooth exceeds the Lipschitz rate. Adversarial robustness. Figure 7 (Appendix G) verifies Corollary 8.2 on closed-form and Adam-trained networks; Figure 8 extends to real data (MNIST, CIFAR-10) at all ε {0.1, 0.3, 1.0, 3.0, 10.0}.

sections.title: 10 Conclusion
sections.content: Every LSE layer is exactly HamiltonJacobi PDE solver (Theorem 4.1), and the single parameter ε closes commutative diagram (Theorem 7.1) that subsumes nearest-neighbor retrieval, convex regularization, and entropic optimal transport as limiting cases of one structure. Generalization, robustness, hallucination geometry, scaling, and Hamiltonian extensions (Theorems 8.1, 8.2, 8.5, 8.8, 4.4) all follow from this single correspondence. The question posed at the outset what equation does neural network solve? has an exact answer; the results above are its first consequences.

In Proceedings of the 35th International Conference on Machine Learning, volume 80 of Proceedings of Machine Learning Research, pages 58245832, 2018. 13 The Physical Analogue: Neural Networks as Path Integrals The HamiltonJacobi equation appearing throughout this paper is not merely convenient analytical device: it is the HamiltonJacobi equation of classical mechanics, tS + H(S) = 0, where is Hamiltons principal function (the action), = is momentum, and is the Hamiltonian. The characteristics of this PDE are Newtons equations of motion. The correspondence between LSE networks and HJ PDEs therefore places neural networks in direct contact with the mathematical structure of physics. Table 1 makes the dictionary precise. Table 1: Dictionary between LSE neural networks and classical/quantum mechanics. Neural network Physics LSE layer at ε > 0 Tropical limit ε 0 ε Softmax weights πj Network forward pass Neurons = 1, . . . , Discrete paths in path integral Quantum statistical mechanics (partition function) Classical mechanics (principle of least action) ℏ (Plancks constant) BoltzmannGibbs probabilities Imaginary-time Schrödinger propagator exp(cid:0)(Wj + bj)/ε(cid:1) is discrete The FeynmanKac formula makes this precise. The quantity (cid:80) path integral: each neuron is path, weighted by the Boltzmann factor ecostj /ε. The network forward pass computes the log-partition function of this ensemble, exactly the imaginary-time Schrödinger propagator xe ˆHt/ℏ evaluated on discrete measure. As ε 0, the sum concentrates on the neuron minimizing the cost, by Laplaces method. In Feynmans formulation of quantum mechanics [1982], the same approximation recovers classical trajectories from the path integral: the phase eiS/ℏ oscillates rapidly away from the classical path, and only the stationary point (the action-minimizing trajectory) survives. The tropical limit ε 0 is the neural-network analogue of ℏ 0: the quantum (soft, probabilistic) computation hardens into the classical (hard, deterministic) max-plus lookup of the HopfLax formula. The parameter ε is therefore simultaneously the softmax temperature, the PDE viscosity, the quadrature regularization scale, and the quantization parameter interpolating between quantum statistical mechanics (ε > 0) and classical mechanics (ε = 0). This is mathematically the same object in the LSE/HopfCole setting: the LSE network, the partition function of Gibbs ensemble, and the imaginary-time Schrödinger propagator are the same mathematical object at different values of ε. Classical simulatability and positive measure. Feynman [1982] established that classical computers cannot efficiently simulate quantum mechanics. The obstruction is structural: the Wigner quasi-probability distribution, which plays the role of phase-space density for quantum states, takes negative values. Since no classical stochastic process can assign negative probabilities, any faithful classical simulation of quantum system requires exponential resources. The LSE framework operates in the classically tractable regime: the Gibbs measure πj(x; ε) exp((Wj + bj)/ε) is genuine probability distribution for all ε > 0. Positivity is what makes the FeynmanKac representation (Proposition E.4) classically computable and distinguishes the present framework from quantum computation. The tropical limit ε 0 concentrates this measure to point mass at the argmax neuron the zero-temperature, maximally classical limit. Cellular automata and ResNets. Feynman [1982] proposed simulating classical physics by locally connected automaton: fixed local update rule, iterated over discrete time steps, with computational cost proportional to the space-time volume. The ResNet recurrence xℓ+1 = xℓ + hF (xℓ, Wℓ) is precisely this structure: the local rule is one HJ characteristic step, iterated times with step size h. Proposition 5.2 and Theorem 8.4 confirm that this automaton computes the characteristics and co-state equations of HJ PDE. The discreteness is not an approximation of continuous limit; it is the structure, as Feynman intended. The layer depth and step size play the roles of discrete time and lattice spacing. 14 Universal quantum simulator vs. universal classical HJ simulator. Feynman [1982] proposed universal quantum simulator: quantum lattice system that can simulate any local quantum field theory with cost proportional to system size, solving the exponential blowup of classical quantum simulation. The LSE network at ε > 0 is the positive-measure counterpart: universal classical simulator for HJ initial-value problems, in the sense of Theorem 8.1, whose O(N 1/d) approximation rate is optimal for Lipschitz data. The two are complementary: the quantum simulator requires quantum hardware because negative Wigner-function values are unavoidable in quantum systems; the classical HJ simulator requires only standard floating-point arithmetic because the Gibbs measure is positive.

sections.title: B Related Work
sections.content: Neural ODEs and continuous-depth networks. [2017] proposed viewing ResNets as discretizations of ODEs; Chen et al. [2018] made this precise with the adjoint method. The ResNet step xl+1 = xl + hF (xl, Wl) is also the EulerMaruyama scheme for controlled SDE [Kloeden and Platen, 1992], identifying the residual network simultaneously as an ODE discretization and as the zero-noise limit of diffusion process. The present work sharpens the connection by identifying the forward ODE as HamiltonJacobi characteristic and the backward pass as its co-state (Theorem 8.4), going beyond the ODE-as-architecture analogy to an exact PDE correspondence. Spline and piecewise-linear theories. Balestriero and Baraniuk [2018, 2021] showed that ReLU networks compute continuous piecewise-affine splines. Montufar et al. [2014] counted linear regions. Aytekin [2022] showed ReLU networks are decision trees. These are the ε = 0 tropical limit of the present framework: max-plus algebra, piecewise linearity, and HopfLax inf-convolution are the same object. The LP characterization of Balestriero and Baraniuk [2021] also connects to sparsity: the tropical argmax selects the single neuron maximizing Wj + bj, which is vertex of the LP feasible set; the one-hot weight vector at ε = 0 is the sparsest nonnegative point in the softmax simplex, in the sense studied by Donoho and Tanner [2005]. Viscosity solutions and HamiltonJacobi PDEs. Classical (smooth) solutions of HJ equations develop shocks in finite time when characteristics cross, so generalized notion of solution is needed. viscosity solution is the unique stable limit that persists through these crossings: is viscosity solution if it satisfies the PDE in one-sided sense at every point via smooth test functions, and the key theorem is that the ε 0 limit of viscous solutions uε always converges to the viscosity solution u0. For ML practitioners the direct analogy is soft-to-hard attention: soft attention (ε > 0, softmax) is the viscous regularization, hard attention (ε = 0, argmax) is the tropical/viscosity limit, and viscosity theory is what makes this limit unique and rigorous. Crandall and Lions [1983] established the viscosity solution theory for HamiltonJacobi equations; Evans [2010] provides the textbook treatment. The HopfCole and HopfLax formulas date to Hopf [1950] and Lax [1957]; their role as exact solutions (rather than approximations) is key to Claims (i) and (iv). The standard reference connecting stochastic optimal control to HJ viscosity solutions is Fleming and Soner [2006]; the extension to viscosity solutions on infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, relevant to the over-parameterized limit, is Lions [1988]. Tropical and max-plus mathematics. Litvinov [2007] surveys Maslov dequantization and the tropical semiring. Tokihiro et al. [1996] introduced ultradiscretization in the context of integrable cellular automata. The connection to neural networks via the ε 0 limit is the main contribution of Section 5. Scaling laws. Kaplan et al. [2020] empirically fit power-law scaling for large language models. The theoretical derivation here (Proposition 8.8) derives the exponent from intrinsic data dimension via quadrature approximation theory, providing mechanistic explanation for the observed scaling. The parameter ε governs activation sparsity (uniform at ε ; one-hot at ε 0); the effect of this variance on training stability in sparse networks is studied by Dent and Tanner [2026]. HJ PDEs for optimization landscapes. Chaudhari et al. [2017] introduced Entropy-SGD, which minimizes the Gibbs-smoothed loss fγ(θ) = β1 log (cid:82) exp(βf (y) θ y2/(2γ)) dy to bias gradient descent toward flat minima; Chaudhari et al. [2018] established that this smoothed loss is the viscous HJ solution in weight space, with the weight vector θ as the PDEs spatial variable and the original loss (θ) as initial data. The present framework is precise role reversal: the spatial variable is the input x, not the weights, and the weights encode the initial data g(yj) of the HJ equation (Theorem 4.1). Chaudhari et al. analyze how the loss landscape deforms under HJ smoothing as weights vary during training; the present paper identifies the network output evaluated at fixed weight setting across all inputs as an exact HJ solution, making inference itself the act of evaluating PDE rather than approximating smoother optimization surface. The MoreauYosida proximal map of their Lemma 2, proxtf (x) = arg miny{f (y) + y2/(2t)}, is precisely the mechanism behind the L2t bias in Theorem 8.1: the HopfLax solution cannot approximate to better than O(t) because the proximal smoothing introduces systematic upward shift of that magnitude. 16 Statistical mechanics and spectral theories of learning. Martin and Mahoney [2021] established Heavy-Tailed Self-Regularization (HTSR): power-law exponents of layer weight matrix spectra predict generalization without access to training or test data, finding formalized in Martin and Hinrichs [2025] (SETOL) using random matrix theory and statistical mechanics. The present framework provides an exact theoretical substrate for this spectral perspective: the viscosity ε governs the spectral scale of via the Hessian bound 2 2,/ε (Corollary 8.2), and the optimal ε 1/d at which the generalization rate O(N 1/d) is attained (Proposition 8.8) provides principled, derivable analogue of the empirical temperature parameter in SETOL. Where SETOL is semi-empirical, fitting spectral observations to RMT predictions, the HJ correspondence grounds the same weight-spectrum-to-generalization link in rigorous PDE identity. xfε2 2 Flow matching and score-based diffusion. Flow matching [Lipman et al., 2023, Liu et al., 2023] learns time-dependent velocity field v(x, t) by minimizing E[v(Xt, t) Xt2] along interpolant paths; the learned field is then integrated as = v(X, t). This is the characteristic ODE of the HJ PDE with Hamiltonian H(x, p) = v(x, t), the same structure as Proposition 5.2 with the drift (x, θ) = v(x, t) identified as the velocity field. Score-based diffusion [Song et al., 2021] trains network to approximate log pt(x). Under the HopfCole substitution, pt(x) = euε(x,t)/ε (up to normalization), so log pt = xuε/ε: the score is the negative normalized spatial gradient of the HopfCole solution, and denoising is gradient ascent on the HJ potential. Both architectures therefore fit within the HJ characteristic framework; the distinction from the feedforward LSE case is that the Hamiltonian is input-dependent and time-varying rather than fixed at H(p) = p2. Infinite-width limits, kernel methods, and beyond. At infinite width, gradient descent is governed by the Neural Tangent Kernel [Jacot et al., 2018] and the network converges to Gaussian process [Lee et al., 2018]. The FeynmanKac representation (Proposition E.4) provides complementary PDE characterization of this limit: the Gibbs average over support points converges to the HopfCole solution of the heat equation, with the matched-scale condition = 2εt identifying the unique Gaussian prior consistent with the kernel bandwidth. For LSE networks, the NTK admits the closed form Kab = ε2π(xa), π(xb) (Proposition D.3): the kernel is the inner product of Gibbs heat-kernel weights, and is positive definite almost surely for generic support points. The HJ framework extends beyond three acknowledged limitations of the NTK perspective. First, NTK theory, operating in the limit of fixed data with width , provides bounds in terms of sample size but does not address how loss scales with model size ; the FeynmanKac quadrature (Theorem 8.1) yields finite-N bound with rate O(N 1/deff ) under the manifold hypothesis (Assumption 8.7), and Proposition 8.8 recovers the empirical exponent α = 1/deff from PDE structure without assuming the kernel regime. Second, in the general training regime where support points yj are also optimized, the Gibbs weights πj(x; θ) are adaptive: the HopfLax minimizer = arg minj{g(yj) + yj2/(4t)} performs energy-weighted nearest-neighbor selection at inference, feature-learning structure that the kernel Gram matrix does not expose. Third, while the NTK depends on architecture, the dependence is through the kernel entries; the HJ framework makes it explicit through Hamiltonian and viscosity ε: feedforward networks have quadratic H, ResNets have general encoding the drift, and Transformers have attention as vector-valued HopfCole transform at viscosity ε = 1/ d. For general wide networks, Dyer and Gur-Ari [2020] derive O(n1) finite-width corrections to the NTK during gradient flow via Feynman-diagram (Gaussian integral) methods; in the LSE case, the closed form Kab = ε2π, π provides an exact PDE interpretation of the kernel that those corrections refine. MoreauYosida envelope and convex analysis. The HopfLax formula u0(x, t) = inf y{g(y) + y2/(4t)} is precisely the Moreau envelope (proximal map) of with quadratic cost λ = 4t [Rockafellar, 1970, Bauschke and Combettes, 2017]. The HopfCole substitution is the unique linearization of this envelope. The present framework recovers and extends the MoreauYosida regularization perspective of convex analysis: where MoreauYosida views entropic-proximal smoothing as tool for optimization, the HJ correspondence identifies it as the architectural primitive of LSE networks. The two perspectives agree at ε = 0 on the HopfLax formula; at ε > 0 the HJ framework lifts this to family of PDE solutions, recovering MoreauYosida as limit. 17 Optimal transport and entropic regularization. Entropic-regularized optimal transport [Peyré and Cuturi, 2019, Cuturi, 2013] shares the LSE/Gibbs-measure structure of the present framework: the Sinkhorn algorithm iterates log-sum-exp operations, and the entropic-OT cost is discrete Hopf Cole log-partition function. The connection is that entropic-OT and the LSE network are the same object from different perspectives one optimizing over couplings, the other over initial data of HJ equation. The soft-min at viscosity ε in the HJ framework is the same Gibbs average that defines the entropic-OT transport plan. Modern Hopfield networks. Ramsauer et al. [2021] connect attention to modern Hopfield networks via the LSE energy function, showing that the softmax attention update rule is the retrieval dynamics of continuous Hopfield network with exponential interactions. Theorem 4.1 provides the exact PDE interpretation of this observation: the Hopfield energy is the HopfCole log-partition function; retrieval is evaluation of the HJ solution at the query point; stored patterns are the support points {yj} of the initial-data measure. Tropical and morphological neural networks. Zhang et al. [2018] study the tropical geometry of deep ReLU networks, showing that the decision boundaries are tropical hypersurfaces. Charisopoulos and Maragos [2017] and Maragos et al. [2021] connect morphological networks to tropical algebra. The present framework positions these results within the HJ picture: the ε = 0 tropical limit is where ReLU and max-pooling operations live (Remark 3.2), and the tropical geometry arises from the HopfLax inf-convolution formula (9). Optimal control and the Pontryagin Maximum Principle. The connection between deep learning and optimal control via the PMP has substantial prior history. [2017] proposed mean-field optimal control for deep learning; Li et al. [2018] made the PMP connection precise for ResNets; Benning et al. [2019] develop general optimal control view of deep learning. Theorem 8.4 sharpens these connections by identifying the forward equation as HJ characteristic ODE, making the joint forward-backward system an exact Hamiltonian flow rather than an optimal-control analogy. Cross-architecture scaling consistency. Shen et al. [2024] observe that scaling law exponents are consistent across linear-complexity architectures and standard Transformers when controlling for data and compute. This is consistent with Proposition 8.8: the exponent α = 1/deff is property of the datas intrinsic dimension, not the architecture. The HJ framework predicts that architectures sharing the same data distribution should exhibit the same scaling exponents, providing theoretical grounding for the empirical cross-architecture consistency. 18 Machine Learning Tasks as Initial-Value Problems Architecture as PDE, Output as Solution In both this framework and physics-informed neural networks (PINNs), the network output is PDE solution: the value uε(x, t) at the input point x. The distinction is not in what the output represents but in where the PDE lives. PINN uses the network as universal function approximator the architecture carries no information about the target PDE. (A ReLU MLP has its own intrinsic tropical HJ structure, but that equation is not the NavierStokes or wave equation being solved.) The target PDE is imposed externally through residual loss tfθ + H(xfθ) εfθ2. The network learns to satisfy the PDE through training; the equation is constraint on learning, not property of the architecture. Here, the PDE structure is in the architecture. The layer ε = LSEε(W + b) encodes the exact HopfCole solution via identity (5), with Hamiltonian determined by the activation function and initial data encoded in the weights (W, b). No residual loss is needed: the PDE residual is zero by construction, and Theorem 4.1 guarantees this before any training begins. The consequence for training is that gradient descent searches over the space of initial conditions {(yj, g(yj))}N j=1, that is, over the family of all viscous HJ equations with discrete initial data, selecting the one whose propagated solution best explains the observations. Tasks as Initial-Value Problems The HopfCole correspondence (Theorem 4.1) is not specific to any one task. Every standard supervised learning task can be cast as an initial-value problem for HJ PDE. The architecture determines the PDE class (the Hamiltonian); the task determines how the independent variables and initial data are interpreted. In all cases the network output is PDE solution value; in all cases training selects the initial conditions. Regression. The output ε (x) = LSEε(W + b) is the HopfCole solution value uε(x, t) at input (Theorem 4.1). Training minimizes loss over x, selecting the initial data that makes uε(, t) best approximate the target function. Classification. K-class classifier computes logits fk(x) = Wkx+bk, one linear HJ characteristic per class. Class probabilities are softmax(f (x)/ε) = LSEε(f (x)): the Gibbs measure over class scores at temperature ε. The predicted class arg maxk fk(x) is the tropical argmax. Decision boundaries are tropical hyperplanes: the piecewise-linear ridges (non-differentiable loci) of LSEε in the tropical limit ε 0. As ε 0, the softmax hardens to one-hot, boundaries sharpen, and the classifier recovers decision tree [Aytekin, 2022]. Classification confidence is ε: low viscosity means sharp, committed decisions; high viscosity means soft, uncertain ones. Kernel Machines. kernel estimator with Gaussian kernel kσ(x, y) = exp(x y2/2σ2) computes (x) = αi exp (cid:18) xi2/2 σ2 (cid:19) . (cid:88) Under the HopfCole substitution = eu/ε, this linear sum equals the transformed variable v(x) = euε(x)/ε of Theorem 4.1 at ε = σ2/2 and = 1, with Gibbs weights αi = eg(xi)/ε. The HJ solution itself is uε(x) = ε log (x): the kernel estimator is the pre-image of the LSE layer under the HopfCole transform, and the two are related by uε = ε log v. As σ 0 (ε 0): nearest-neighbor classification, the tropical argmax limit [Litvinov, 2007]. Time Series. The sequence index is the PDE time variable. An RNN, LSTM, or SSM integrating time series solves an ODE = (h, x(t)) along (Proposition 5.4). The hidden state ht is the PDE state; the prediction ˆxt+1 is the solution value at time + 1. Learning the model is learning the Hamiltonian whose characteristics match the observed trajectory. 19 Sequences (Transformers). Token embedding plays the role of the spatial variable x; transformer depth plays the role of PDE time t. Each attention layer is one step of the HJ propagator St (Theorem 4.1). The contextualized embedding at layer is the HJ solution at time = t, with token embeddings as initial data [Tong et al., 2025]. EncoderDecoder. The encoder applies the propagator St1 : latent = St1[g](x) is the PDE solution at intermediate time t1. The decoder applies second propagator St2: output ˆy = St2[z](x). Together they form the composed semigroup St1+t2 (Claim (ii) of Theorem 7.1). The bottleneck is the PDE state at the encoderdecoder boundary; its dimension is the effective number of degrees of freedom of the initial-data measure at that time.

Next state Contextual emb. 20 Regularity Scope and the SGDInitial-Condition Connection Regularity scope of Theorem 7.1 The five claims of Theorem 7.1 have distinct regularity requirements, reflecting fundamental separation between the identification of neural networks with PDE solutions and the approximation theory governing convergence. ε (x) = x2/(4t) uN Claims (i) and (ii): Unconditional. The identity ε (x, t) is pure algebraic identity holding for any weights Wj and biases bj, with no assumption on the implied initial data g, the Hamiltonian H, or the input distribution. The proof is completing-the-square manipulation (Appendix E, Theorem 4.1) that never invokes regularity. The semigroup tower property (Claim (ii)) follows from the HopfCole substitution = eu/ε reducing the HJ equation to the heat equation tv = εv; the linearity and semigroup property of the heat equation hold without any regularity on the initial data. The consequence is decisive: for any trained neural network with arbitrary weights, there exists an exact viscous HJ PDE for which the network layer is the exact solution. This is not an asymptotic statement and does not require data or initialization assumptions. Claim (iii): Lipschitz required. The quadrature bound uN ε uε CLN 1/d (where = Lip(g)) requires Lip(L). The constant is uniform in ε, which is what allows Claim (v) to hold. For or BV, viscosity solution theory [Crandall and Lions, 1983, Evans, 2010] still guarantees existence and uniqueness of uε, but the O(N 1/d) quadrature rate may not be achievable and the approximation theory must be revisited. Claim (iv): Convex, superlinear required. Pointwise convergence uε u0 as ε 0 is established via the HopfLax formula (Theorem 5.1), which is valid precisely when is convex and superlinear. Under these conditions, u0 is the unique viscosity solution of the inviscid HJ equation and equals the HopfLax inf-convolution. For non-convex H, viscosity solutions of the inviscid equation still exist by Crandall and Lions [1983], but the HopfLax representation need not hold and Claim (iv) requires separate argument. Claim (v): Both conditions required. Commutativity of the limits and ε 0 inherits the conditions of both Claims (iii) and (iv): Lipschitz ensures the quadrature error is uniform in ε, and convex superlinear ensures the viscosity convergence is uniform in . SGD as initial-condition optimization in the mean-field limit The Limitations section of the main text notes that the framework characterizes what trained network computes (the optimal initial-value problem) but not how stochastic gradient descent selects it. The mean-field theory of neural networks [Mei et al., 2018] closes this gap in the infinite-width limit. In the mean-field scaling (width with 1/N output normalization), the network function converges to fµ(x) = ε log (cid:90) (cid:16) exp g(y) + y2/(4t) ε Rd (cid:17) dµ(y), (20) where µ P(Rd) is probability measure over support points, the infinite-width limit of the empirical measure µN = 1 δyj . This is exactly the HopfCole solution (3) under the continuous measure µ. Training by gradient descent in this limit corresponds to the L2-Wasserstein gradient flow of the population risk R[µ] = E(x,y)D[ℓ(fµ(x), y)]: (cid:80) tµt = W2 R[µt]. (21) Equation (21) is the gradient flow over the space of initial-data measures for the HJ equation. The measure µ specifies which initial-value problem the network solves; gradient descent moves µ in the direction that decreases the risk. Proposition D.1 (SGD selects the initial-value problem). In the mean-field limit , stochastic gradient descent on the network parameters (Wj, bj) converges in distribution to the Wasserstein gradient flow (21) of the population risk. Each gradient step moves the support points yj and initial values g(yj) in the direction that decreases R, identifying the optimization trajectory as the search for the risk-minimizing initial-value problem of the viscous HJ equation. 21 Proof. The result is structural: it identifies the HJ interpretation of the mean-field limit established by Mei et al. [2018] for two-layer networks with 1/N normalization. Under that result, gradient flow on the empirical risk converges as to the McKeanVlasov equation tµt = W2R[µt]. Under the parametrization Wj = yj/(2t), bj = g(yj) yj2/(4t), each neuron encodes support point (yj, g(yj)) of the initial-data measure; the limiting flow is therefore the Wasserstein gradient flow on that measure, i.e., the search over initial-value problems (21). The identification applies to two-layer networks under the conditions of Mei et al. [2018]; extensions to deeper networks or SGD on empirical risk require additional propagation-of-chaos arguments. The connection to Theorem 8.4 (backpropagation as the co-state equation) is direct: the co-state variable pl specifies the optimal direction to update (yj, g(yj)) at each layer, which is the singlestep Euler approximation of the Wasserstein gradient direction (21). For finite , the particle approximation introduces an error of order O(N 1/d) by Claim (iii) of Theorem 7.1the same rate that controls the PDE approximation error. The gap between the mean-field and finite-N regimes is thus governed by the same quantity as the correspondences approximation theory, providing unified error budget for both the PDE identification and the optimization interpretation. Kim and Suzuki [2024] carry out related mean-field analysis for transformer training via Wasserstein gradient flow on the attention landscape, proving that the loss landscape over parameters, while nonconvex, is algorithmically benign. The nonconvexity there is in the optimization landscape over weights; the open problem noted in the Limitations concerns non-convex Hamiltonians in the PDE, structurally different obstruction at the level of the PDE geometry rather than the parameter space. sections.title: G Numerical Details
sections.content: All experiments run on standard CPU; no GPU is required. Shared defaults. All trained networks use Adam (β1 = 0.9, β2 = 0.999, ˆε = 108); weights initialized as Wj (0, 0.12/d), biases bj = 0; viscosity ε = 1/d per Theorem 8.1. Experiment (initial-data recovery): Adam warm-start (5,000 steps, lr = 0.008) followed by L-BFGS-B; = 10 neurons, 8 seeds (best reported), 1-D synthetic g(y) = on [2.4, 2.4], ε {0.5, 0.1, 0.04}. Experiment (scaling law): Adam, 3,000 steps, lr = 0.006, 5 seeds (best reported), train/test 4,000/800, synthetic g(y) = y2 on [2, 2]d; = 1: {10, 25, 50, 100, 200, 500}; = 2: {20, 50, 100, 200, 500}; = 4: {40, 100, 200, 500, 1000}. The deformation ε is formally identical to ℏ in Maslovs dequantization: the passage ε 0 is the neural-network analogue of the ℏ 0 classical limit [Feynman, 1982]. The LSE network at ε > 0 is universal classical HJ simulator (Theorem 8.1) the positive-measure counterpart of the universal quantum simulator of Feynman [1982]; classical realizability holds precisely because the Gibbs measure is positive for all ε > 0, unlike the Wigner quasi-distribution of quantum mechanics. Universality. The exact correspondence (Theorem 4.1) applies to LSE-activated networks. The following proposition shows the class is large enough to be universal. Proposition H.1 (LSE universality). Let Rd be compact and : continuous. For every δ > 0 there exists deep LSE network fε such that supxK (x) fε(x) < δ. Any continuous on compact is uniformly approximable by continuous piecewise-linear function (StoneWeierstrass on K). Every continuous piecewise-linear function can be written in max-of-min-of-affine form, which depth-two tropical (max-plus/min-plus) network represents exactly; this follows from the lattice form of the StoneWeierstrass theorem [Litvinov, 2007], since max-plus/min-plus functions separate points and form function lattice. For fixed ε > 0, each max is approximated by LSEε to within O(ε log ) (Theorem 3.1), and each min by ε log (cid:80) ezj /ε to the same order. Choosing ε and appropriately gives the result. Together, Theorem 4.1 and Proposition H.1 imply: for any continuous function there exists deep viscous HJ solution under discrete measures that approximates it to within δ. The HJ class is therefore as general as the class of continuous functions (with depth 2). Closure. Proposition H.2 (Closure of the HJ class). The class of functions representable as HopfCole solutions of viscous HJ equations under discrete measures is closed under: (i) Layer composition: composing LSE layers corresponds to applying the heat semigroup for ℓ tℓ (Theorem 7.1); the result is another HJ solution. (ii) Affine input transformations: fε(Ax + c) is an LSE network with weights and biases total time = (cid:80) + c, hence still an exact HJ solution. (iii) Residual connections: adding skip path corresponds to the Euler discretization of HJ characteristics (Proposition 5.2); the composition remains within the HJ class up to discretization error O(h). Extension to broader PDE classes. When the Hamiltonian admits HopfLax or inf-convolution representation (convex H), the correspondence extends to scalar conservation laws, eikonal equations, and linear transport; in each case the network encodes the Hamiltonian in its weights and the viscosity in ε. The KdV and Toda lattice hierarchies ultradiscretize to box-ball cellular automata [Tokihiro et al., 1996], suggesting connection for non-convex Hamiltonians, but the neural network analogue in that setting remains open. Remark H.3 (Non-convex Hamiltonians: obstructions and partial results). Convexity of enters the framework at two algebraic points. First, the HopfCole substitution = eu/ε linearizes tu + H(u) = εu to the heat equation for only when is quadratic (Theorem 4.1) or learnable quadratic form (Theorem 4.4); for general non-quadratic H, the substitution introduces nonlinear reaction term that does not vanish, so the exact algebraic identity ε + uε = x2/(4t) breaks. Second, the LegendreFenchel conjugate = is always convex (as pointwise supremum of affine functions), so the HopfLax formula always produces well-defined viscosity solution but for the convex envelope = (H ), not for the original non-convex H; the two solutions differ by O(t ) (Theorem H.5(ii)). The kernel network ε of Theorem H.5 is convex in x: since = is convex, the map (cid:55) tL((x yj)/t) is convex for each j, and log-sum-exp preserves convexity. Single-layer networks are therefore limited to representing solutions that are convex functions of the input, which excludes non-convex HJ solutions. In practice, LSE networks have depth 2, which is required for universal approximation of non-convex targets (Proposition H.1). Applying the kernel construction of Theorem H.5 layer-by-layer via the multi-step HJ semigroup (Theorem 7.1), with per-layer time steps tℓ = t/L, yields deep LSE network in which each layer is individually convex in its own input but the composition over 2 layers is not globally convex, recovering the non-convex function class required for non-convex HJ solutions. Three consequences follow. First, the Gaussian kernel used in kernel methods [Jacot et al., 2018] and in the NTK literature is the heat kernel at unit time; LSE networks are therefore kernel machines whose kernel is the fundamental solution of the heat equation. Second, the limit (Theorem 7.1(iii)) recovers the continuum Greens function integral, connecting neural scaling to the convergence of quadrature rules for the fundamental solution. Third, the tropical limit (ε 0) replaces the heat kernel with the Dirac mass δy(x) concentrated at the minimizer y(x) = arg minj{x yj2/(4t) + g(yj)} (Varadhans lemma, [Varadhan, 1984]), recovering the HopfLax formula and connecting to nearest-neighbor / Voronoi classifiers. Translation equivariance corresponds to shift-invariance of the initial-data measure: the filter defines one viscous HJ equation whose HopfCole solution is evaluated position-wise. By Noethers theorem, the position-independence of H(p) = p2 implies that the co-state = xu is conserved along each characteristic ( = xH = 0); parameter sharing in CNNs corresponds to imposing this conservation globally, constraining gradients to be the same filter across all spatial positions. sections.title: Intrinsic Dimension from Published Scaling Curves
sections.content: Proposition 8.8 predicts L(N ) 1/deff , so the empirical scaling exponent α directly estimates the intrinsic dimension of the data-generating measure via deff = 1/α. Table 7 applies this to published scaling curves across four domains. The Kaplan and Chinchilla language exponents differ because Kaplan et al. [2020] under-train large models (insufficient data), inflating the apparent dimension; Hoffmann et al. [2022] correct this with compute-optimal allocation and recover lower, more interpretable deff 2.9. The video and math exponents from Henighan et al. [2020] use model-size scaling at convergence (Figure 3 of that paper). Table 7: Empirical scaling exponent α and implied intrinsic dimension deff = 1/α from published scaling curves. All exponents are for loss vs. model parameter count . The conversion deff = 1/α is directional: empirical α conflates approximation, optimization, and data-coverage effects, so the values should be interpreted as order-of-magnitude estimates rather than exact intrinsic dimensions. In particular, the Kaplan exponent reflects under-training rather than the data manifold alone; the Chinchilla exponent is more interpretable as geometric quantity. Domain Source α deff = 1/α Kaplan et al. [2020] Language (GPT-scale, under-trained) Language (compute-optimal) Hoffmann et al. [2022] Video (16 frames, 1616 VQ; Fig. 3 of Henighan et al. 2020) Henighan et al. [2020] Henighan et al. [2020] Math 0.076 0.35 0.24 0.38 13.2 2.9 4.2 2.6 The variation in deff across domains is consistent with the manifold hypothesis: mathematical problem solving has lower intrinsic dimension than language (structured, fewer degrees of freedom), while video has intermediate dimension.

sections.title: J Actionable Design Principles
sections.content: The theoretical correspondences established in the main body translate into concrete prescriptions for practitioners working with LSE networks or related architectures. The implications below are derived directly from the theorems and are not empirical claims about performance at scale. Optimal temperature selection. Theorem 8.1 identifies the minimax-optimal viscosity ε 1/d as the value minimizing the excess risk O(N 1/d + (cid:112)N/n) jointly over approximation and estimation error. This is closed-form prescription: given network of width and data of intrinsic dimension (estimable from scaling curves via Proposition 8.8), the optimal temperature follows from and alone, with no grid search. Setting ε below ε under-regularizes (estimation error dominates); setting ε above ε over-smooths (approximation error dominates). Robustness-accuracy trade-off. Corollary 8.2 shows 2 2,/ε for all inputs, independently of the weights. The worst-case sensitivity to input perturbations is therefore certifiably controlled by ε: increasing ε monotonically decreases perturbation sensitivity, while decreasing ε toward ε sharpens discrimination. Practitioners facing adversarial robustness requirements can therefore raise ε to obtain certified guarantee from Corollary 8.2 without retraining. ε 2 2 xf Temperature annealing. Since ε controls approximation quality (via 1/d) and robustness (via 2 2,/ε) simultaneously, it admits principled annealing schedule. Starting training at high ε provides smooth, well-conditioned loss landscape; gradually annealing toward ε sharpens the approximation as training stabilizes. This is the neural-network analogue of PDE continuation methods, and the framework supplies the theoretical justification: the schedule follows the path along which the HJ equation transitions from the strongly viscous regime (diffusion-dominated) to the mildly viscous regime near optimal viscosity. Architecture as discretization strategy. All four architecture classes are different discretizations of the same HJ equation, making the choice of architecture choice of discretization strategy rather than an empirical design decision. Feedforward networks discretize the measure: neurons are iid samples from the initial-data measure with drift = 0. ResNets discretize the process via EulerMaruyama with drift = (x, ); the layer-to-layer dynamics are the characteristics of the HJ equation. Transformers use attention as vector-valued HopfCole average (Proposition H.9); the attention weights are the Gibbs measure at temperature ε = dk. Recurrent architectures discretize linear or nonlinear characteristics with architecture-dependent viscosity (Proposition 5.4). Tasks with natural temporal ordering (sequence modeling, time series) favor process-discretization strategies (ResNet, recurrent); tasks requiring an invariant of static distribution favor measurediscretization (feedforward); tasks requiring query-dependent weighting of stored data favor attention. Backpropagation as adjoint optimization. Theorem 8.4 identifies backpropagation as the co-state equation of the Hamiltonian system associated with the HJ PDE. The adjoint method is the standard technique for gradient computation in PDE-constrained optimization; the present result establishes that standard reverse-mode autodifferentiation is already an instance of it. This opens the design space for training algorithms: symplectic integrators (which preserve the Hamiltonian structure exactly) and higher-order ODE solvers can replace the Euler gradient step with theoretical guarantees on conservation of the Hamiltonian, potentially improving long-horizon training stability. Compute-optimal scaling forecasts. Proposition 8.8 identifies the scaling exponent α = 1/deff from L(N ) α, so the data intrinsic dimension can be read directly from published scaling curves. Conversely, given an estimate of deff from small pilot run, the framework predicts the full scaling curve and the compute-optimal allocation nd/(d+2) before large training run, allowing resource allocation to be grounded in the approximation-rate theory rather than extrapolation of empirical trend lines. 47 Implications for Large-Scale Training, Alignment, and Continual Learning The framework yields theorem-grounded observations on three questions of practical importance; the implications are derived from the approximation bounds and structural results established above, and are not claims about empirical performance at scale. Internet-scale data exhaustion. Theorem 8.1 decomposes the excess risk as O(N 1/deff + (cid:112)N/n): the first term is the approximation error (model too small to resolve the data measure) and the second is the estimation error (data too scarce to pin down the measure). At fixed dataset size = nmax (the internet fully crawled), the estimation floor (cid:112)N/nmax cannot be reduced by adding more data. The bottleneck shifts from measure coverage to two model-side quantities: width (more neurons approximate the measure more finely, at rate 1/deff ) and intrinsic dimension deff (lower dimension means fewer neurons are needed for given accuracy). Data scaling is therefore not over but transformed: the gain from doubling becomes negligible once (deff +2)/deff , at which point architectural inductive bias (reducing deff ) dominates. Remark K.1 (The era of small models). The framework implies that brute-force scaling of at fixed deff is the least efficient path: halving the approximation error requires 2deff more neurons, so at deff = 13 (Kaplan-era GPT, Table 7) each halving costs factor of 8,192 in model size. The sustainable alternative is to reduce deff directly, which is exponentially more efficient. Four levers follow from the framework. (i) Architectural inductive bias. Imposing symmetries reduces the effective support dimension. The CNN analysis of Remark H.8 is precise instance: translation equivariance enforces shiftinvariance of the initial-data measure, collapsing d-dimensional problem to K-dimensional one (receptive field size d). By Noethers theorem, every such symmetry conserves co-state quantity and reduces deff by the dimension of the symmetry group. (ii) Data curation. Raw internet data has deff 13  (Table 7)  ; curated domain-specific corpora have deff 2.6 (math) to 2.9 (compute-optimal language). Each halving of the approximation error costs 2deff more neurons; at deff = 13 vs. deff = 3 the same error halving requires 213/23 = 210 1,000 times more neurons. (iii) Domain specialization. model restricted to low-deff domain is small model that outperforms large general model on that domain, since the general model must allocate neurons to cover the full high-dimensional support. (iv) Sparse activation (MoE and distillation). In the tropical limit ε 0, the network routes all mass to single neuron (argmax). Mixture-of-experts architectures approximate this by routing inputs to specialized sub-networks, each covering low-deff region of the input space. Distillation transfers the initial-data measure of large network into smaller one with < neurons; the information loss is O(N 1/deff ) O(N 1/deff ), which is small when deff is low. The Chinchilla result [Hoffmann et al., 2022] is already case in point: compute-optimal training recovered deff 2.9 from the deff 13 of the under-trained Kaplan regime, matching or exceeding GPT-3-scale performance at fraction of the parameter count. The framework predicts that further reductions in deff through the levers above will continue to shrink the model sizes needed for given capability level. The era of planetary-scale compute is the consequence of optimizing the wrong variable; the approximation-rate analysis suggests architectural inductive bias is the highest-leverage axis for reducing model size at fixed accuracy. Alignment. Proposition D.1 identifies training as the selection of the risk-minimizing initial-value problem: the loss R[µ] determines which initial data the network encodes. Alignment is thus the problem of designing so the risk-minimizing IVP encodes human-valued initial data. The hallucination result (Proposition 8.5) identifies the structural failure mode: in out-of-distribution regions where (x)/ε log , the output is exponentially close to the dominant neurons linear extrapolation and is structurally ungoverned by R. No loss function can align behavior in regions outside the support of the training measure, regardless of its form. Three interventions follow directly from the framework. Data coverage: expanding the support of µ (diverse training data) is the primary lever, since every in-distribution point is governed by R. Viscosity control: increasing ε smooths the OOD extrapolation (the Hessian bound of Corollary 8.2 ensures the output changes slowly with ε), reducing confidence on unfamiliar inputs. OOD penalties: augmenting with penalties at 48 synthetic OOD points moves those points into the training support. The measure-poisoning analysis (Proposition E.3) shows that adversarial neuron insertion shifts the output by softplusε(z0 ε ), with the viscosity parameter ε controlling the damage ceiling. (cid:80) Continual learning. trained network encodes the discrete measure µN = 1 δyj . Learning new task corresponds to adding new support points to µN ; catastrophic forgetting corresponds to their displacement. When gradient descent on new data overwrites existing neurons, the coverage of µN for the old task shrinks. The quadrature bound of Theorem 7.1(iii) quantifies the damage: if of the neurons previously covering task 1 are reassigned, the approximation error for task 1 grows from O(N 1/deff ) to O((N k)1/deff ). The framework prescribes principled remedy: allocate additional neurons for new tasks rather than displacing old ones, growing proportionally to the cumulative number of tasks. Parameter-isolation methods (freezing neurons after learning) correspond to fixing the support points {yj} of the old tasks measure; generative replay corresponds to re-introducing old support points into the training measure so that gradient descent does not displace them. Both strategies have the same PDE interpretation: preserving the coverage of µN for previously learned tasks.

sections.title: L Discussion and Limitations
sections.content: Limitations Training dynamics and the selection problem. The abstract identifies training as search through HamiltonJacobi initial-value problems; this is precise for any fixed parameterization (each trained network is an HJ initial datum, Theorem 4.1), but the gradient-step interpretation requires qualification. The framework characterizes what converged or optimally parameterized LSE network computes (the HopfCole solution of viscous HJ equation under discrete initial measure) but not how stochastic gradient descent selects this initial-value problem from data. Proposition D.1 (Appendix D) establishes mean-field correspondence: gradient flow on the two-layer Gibbs energy functional converges to the optimal support measure under the MeiMontanari conditions [Mei et al., 2018]. For finite , Proposition D.3 (Appendix D) shows that the neural tangent kernel of an LSE network takes the closed form Kab = π(xa), π(xb) the inner product of Gibbs weight vectors and is positive definite almost surely for generic support points, guaranteeing convergence to zero training error at linear rate under MSE. This connection is precise but narrow: it applies to two-layer networks in the infinite-width mean-field limit, with i.i.d. Gaussian initialization and specific loss functional. For deep networks (L 2), practical mini-batch SGD, and non-Gaussian data, the training trajectory is not characterized by the current framework. The Pontryagin Maximum Principle interpretation of backpropagation (Theorem 8.4) describes the gradient of the loss with respect to the initial datum, but which initial datum gradient descent converges to, among the many local minima of the HJ landscape, remains open. Non-quadratic Hamiltonians. The exact HopfCole identity (Theorem 4.1) holds if and only if the Hamiltonian is quadratic, H(p) = p2 or its anisotropic extension pAθp (Theorem 4.4); the quadratic class is maximal for exact identities (Theorem 4.3). For non-quadratic H, the framework provides two partial results: (i) convex-envelope approximation uε,H uε,H with explicit error bounds controlled by the convexity gap (Appendix H); and (ii) structural correspondences for specific activations (ReLU, GELU, SiLU, sigmoid; Table 6) that identify the Hamiltonian but do not give an exact closed-form identity. Which activation functions admit an exact HopfCole identity for some H, and whether any do beyond the quadratic class, is open. Curse of dimensionality. The rate O(N 1/d) is minimax-optimal for Lipschitz functions in dimensions (Theorem 8.1), so the framework provides no escape from the curse: achieving error δ requires δd neurons. The intrinsic-dimension hypothesis (Assumption 8.7) replaces with deff when data concentrates near submanifold, but deff = 1/α inferred from scaling exponents is an empirical estimate, not certified bound on the support geometry. Integrable hierarchy: partial connection. The Kadomtsev-Petviashvili (KP) hierarchy is classical family of integrable nonlinear wave equations encompassing KdV, the Toda lattice, and related systems; exact solutions are organized by tau-functions, scalar generating functions whose logarithm recovers the physical wave field. The elementary solutions are solitons: stable, localized wave packets characterized by wavenumber kj, which travel without dispersing and interact by elastic scattering, each two-soliton collision producing only phase shift encoded by an interaction factor Aij. Proposition M.1 places the -neuron LSE partition function in the free-soliton sector of this hierarchy: each neuron contributes an independent mode with wavenumber kj, but the nontrivial Hirota phase shifts that characterize genuine -soliton interactions are absent. The full -soliton tau-function requires pairwise interaction factors Aij = ((ki kj)/(ki + kj))2; whether trained weights reproduce these (i.e., whether gradient descent learns soliton scattering or only the free-particle sector) remains open, and positive answer would connect weight optimization to the inverse scattering transform. Architectural scope. The exact HopfCole correspondence covers LSE-activated feedforward networks and the specific L2-normalized attention mechanism of Proposition 4.2. Several practically important architectures lie outside the exact framework: (i) Multi-head attention splits the query keyvalue space into heads; the HJ interpretation applies to each head independently, but the concatenation and projection step does not have clean HJ formulation. (ii) Causal (autoregressive) masking restricts the softmax to triangular attention pattern; this breaks the spatial isotropy of the Gaussian heat kernel, and the HopfCole solution no longer applies without modification. (iii) ReLU attention and linear attention replace the softmax with different normalization; the resulting Hamiltonian is non-quadratic and falls under the non-quadratic limitation above. (iv) GELU and SiLU activations yield structural correspondences (the Hamiltonian is identified but not quadratic) rather than exact identities; the error relative to the nearest exact LSE network is bounded but not zero. Extended discussion of related work. Joint-embedding predictive architectures (JEPA) [LeCun, 2022] fit the same structure. Each encoder is HJ semigroup evaluation (Theorem 4.1); the predictor is transport map between two HJ solutions. At ε = 0 the optimal predictor is the Brenier map characterized by the HopfLax formula (Theorem 5.1); at ε > 0 entropic regularization replaces HopfLax with the HopfCole dual potential. The EMA target encoder and stop-gradient carry the structure of mean-field game over the embedding space. What the HJ correspondence provides is the language in which these perspectives are seen to be aspects of one mathematical object, parameterized by the MaslovLitvinov deformation ε [Litvinov, 2007]: the passage from the smooth arithmetic semiring to the tropical one. The framework does not replace existing perspectives; it locates them. The parameter ε plays three simultaneous roles (softmax temperature, PDE viscosity, and proximal regularization strength), made exact by Theorem 7.1; the robustness bound of Corollary 8.2, the optimal viscosity ε 1/deff , and the bifurcation landscape of Theorem 8.9 are all consequences.
